metadata.source_file: UHC Botulinum Toxins A and B – Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy.pdf

coverage_rules.notes: Coverage reviews may be in place if required by law or the benefit plan. Refer to the Medical Benefit Drug Policy titled Medical Benefit Therapeutic Equivalent Medications – Excluded Drugs and the corresponding excluded drug list with preferred alternatives.

criteria: Diagnosis
Botulinum toxin administration is no more frequent than every 12 weeks, regardless of diagnosis
Prescriber attests dosing is in accordance with the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved labeling
For indications without FDA approved dosing, prescriber attests there is published clinical evidence to support the dosing
coverage_rules.notes: For requests that require medical necessity review, also refer to the General Requirements and Diagnosis-Specific Requirements sections below. For Medicare reviews, refer to the CMS section.

criteria: Diagnosis
Botulinum toxin administration is no more frequent than every 12 weeks, regardless of diagnosis
Prescriber attests dosing is in accordance with the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved labeling
For indications without FDA approved dosing, prescriber attests there is published clinical evidence to support the dosing
coverage_rules.notes: For requests that require medical necessity review, also refer to the General Requirements and Diagnosis-Specific Requirements sections below. For Medicare reviews, refer to the CMS section.

OnabotulinumtoxinA is beneficial for the prophylaxis of chronic migraine headaches based upon FDA approval, published practice guidelines, professional society evidence reviews, randomized controlled clinical trials, and smaller randomized exploratory studies.

Clinical evidence presented showing improvement in overactive bladder symptoms vs placebo (p < 0.05) and improved patient health related quality of life (p < 0.001). Most common adverse event was uncomplicated urinary tract infection with 5.4% rate of urinary retention observed.

Clinical evidence presented from randomized placebo-controlled study showing significant improvement in Modified Ashworth Scale scores at week 4 with doses of 10 U/kg/leg and 15 U/kg/leg. Physician's Global Assessment also significantly improved. Most common treatment-related adverse event was muscular weakness. Subgroup analysis showed similar efficacy in both treatment-naive and previously treated patients.

Clinical evidence presented from randomized placebo-controlled trial with 315 subjects (200 U BoNT-A vs placebo). No difference in I-PSS between groups at primary endpoint (week 12) or other time points. Authors concluded BoNT-A is unlikely to be a therapy for male LUTS/BPH. Phase III trial in 2018 showed mixed results with placebo effect concerns.

Four studies reviewed from American Academy of Neurology's 2008 assessment. Mixed results with primary efficacy endpoints not met in largest study (n=702). Some doses (225 U and 150 U) showed statistically significant greater reductions in headache frequency compared with placebo at day 240 (p = 0.03), but placebo response was higher than expected. Further studies recommended due to mixed results.

Four studies reviewed from American Academy of Neurology's 2008 assessment. Most studies showed no significant benefit or BoNT not more beneficial than placebo. One smaller trial showed reduction in headache severity and increased headache-free days. Additional small randomized controlled trials found conflicting results. Until larger randomized trials show beneficial effect, use in tension headache is unproven.

2014 Cochrane review comparing endoscopic pneumatic dilation versus botulinum toxin injection. No significant difference in remission within four weeks. At six months and 12 months, pneumatic dilation showed superior remission rates (risk ratio 1.57 at 6 months, 1.88 at 12 months). No serious adverse outcomes in botulinum group. Authors concluded pneumatic dilation is more effective long-term treatment.

2017 Cochrane review update of 8 RCTs with 1010 participants. Doses 120 U to 240 U. BtA associated with moderate-to-large improvement in baseline clinical status with reduction of 8.06 points in TWSTRS total score at week 4 (95% CI 6.08 to 10.05), corresponding to 18.7% improvement from baseline. Dysphagia (9%) and diffuse weakness/tiredness (10%) were most common treatment-related adverse events. Moderate certainty in evidence.

2018 Cochrane review of 28 studies with 4,190 participants evaluating botulinum toxins versus placebo or active treatment for prevention or reduction in frequency of chronic or episodic migraine. Longest treatment duration was three rounds of injections with three months between treatments. Most trials (21 out of 28) were small with fewer than 50 participants per trial arm. Twenty-three trials compared botulinum toxin with placebo.

Evidence shows reduction of 3.1 days (95% CI -4.7 to -1.4, low-quality evidence) or -2 days (95% CI -2.8 to -1.1, moderate-quality evidence) when small trials removed. Reduces headache days per month by 1.9 days (95% CI -2.7 to -1.0, high-quality evidence). Adverse event risk ratio 1.28 (95% CI 1.12 to 1.47, moderate-quality evidence).

Single trial (n = 418) showed no difference between Botulinum Toxins A and B for this outcome measure (p = 0.49). Authors remain uncertain whether treatment is effective due to very low quality evidence.

Hayes rating C (potential but unproven benefit). Many placebo-controlled RCTs found BTX-A did not provide statistically significant benefits or found inconsistent benefits. Largest RCT found statistically significant improvements in migraine frequency and severity. Benefits obtained with onaBTX-A were relatively small, perhaps too small to be clinically significant. Insufficient evidence regarding efficacy relative to other types of medication for prevention of migraine. Lack of long-term follow-up data. Overall safe with few serious complications reported.
Hayes rating D (no proven benefit and/or not safe). Very limited evidence regarding effectiveness of aboBTX-A for management of chronic or recurrent headache.
Hayes rating D (no proven benefit and/or not safe). No evidence regarding other types of BTX for management of chronic or recurrent headache.

Hayes Medical Technology Directory dated December 30, 2011. Majority of well-designed, randomized, placebo-controlled trials found no benefit of BTX-A relative to placebo. Two studies reporting beneficial effects were very small. Overall BTX-A was safe. No studies compared BTX-A with other prophylactic treatments for CTTH. Annual review January 13, 2015 resulted in no changes.

Hayes Medical Technology Directory dated December 30, 2011. Some evidence that onaBTX-A improves outcomes; however, studies do not provide sufficient evidence to establish clinical role. RCTs consistently found BTX-A provided statistically significant improvements in urinary incontinence compared with placebo, but largest RCT found placebo effect nearly as large as treatment effect. No long-term follow-up studies of multiple doses. No studies compared BTX-A with augmentation cystoplasty or neuromodular implantation. At least six studies sponsored by manufacturer. Additional controlled studies needed.

Largest available RCT found BTX-A treatment associated with statistically significant increases in urinary retention and urinary tract infections. No long-term follow-up studies of multiple doses. No studies compared BTX-A with augmentation cystoplasty or neuromodular implantation. At least six studies sponsored by manufacturer. Additional controlled studies needed.

2017 update to 2012 Cochrane review. Six RCTs graded as low and very low-certainty evidence showed varying responses. Lack of evidence for effect on reducing visual symptoms in acute sixth nerve palsy. Poor response in people with horizontal strabismus without binocular vision. Similar or slightly reduced achievement of successful ocular alignment in children with esotropia. Potential increased achievement of successful ocular alignment where surgery and botulinum toxin combined. Further high-quality trials required.

coverage_rules.indication_name: motor and phonic tics in Tourette's syndrome
coverage_rules.notes: 2018 Cochrane review. Only one randomized placebo-controlled, double-blind cross-over study met selection criteria. 20 participants enrolled, 18 completed (14 with Tourette's syndrome diagnosis); 21 focal motor tics treated. Small number of participants with relatively mild tics. Effects on tic frequency and premonitory urge uncertain (very low-quality evidence). Nine people had muscle weakness following injection. No data on immunoresistance. Authors uncertain about botulinum toxin effects; additional randomized controlled studies needed.

American Academy of Otolaryngology – Head and Neck Surgery 2018 update (guideline first published 2009). Recommendation means benefits exceed harms but quality of evidence not as strong (grade B or C). Clinicians should generally follow recommendation but remain alert to new information and sensitive to patient preferences.

criteria: Recommend botulinum toxin injection as first-line therapy for patients with achalasia who are unfit for definitive therapies compared with other less effective pharmacological therapies (Grade quality: Moderate; Recommendation strength: Strong)
Previous treatment with botulinum toxin injection does not significantly affect performance and outcomes of myotomy (Grade quality: Low; Recommendation strength: Conditional)
coverage_rules.notes: American College of Gastroenterology 2020 evidence-based clinical guideline. For patients with achalasia candidates for definite therapy: Pneumatic dilation, laparoscopic Heller myotomy, and peroral endoscopic myotomy are comparable effective therapies for type I or type II achalasia. POEM better treatment option for type III achalasia. Botulinum toxin injection reserved for those who cannot undergo above definitive therapies.

program_exceptions: Many summary plan descriptions explicitly exclude benefit coverage for medical and surgical treatment of excessive sweating (hyperhidrosis). The member specific benefit plan document must be reviewed to determine what benefits, if any, exist for treatment of hyperhidrosis.

Some Certificates of Coverage allow for coverage of experimental/investigational/unproven treatments for life-threatening illnesses when certain conditions are met. The member specific benefit plan document must be consulted to make coverage decisions for this service.

Some states mandate benefit coverage for off-label use of medications for some diagnoses or under some circumstances when certain conditions are met. Where such mandates apply, they supersede language in the benefit document or in the medical or drug policy.
extraction_notes: This is a coverage policy document with clinical evidence sections. The source text does not contain specific ICD-10 codes, HCPCS codes, or NDC codes.

Daxxify® is listed as 'typically excluded from coverage' but also has clinical evidence supporting its use for Cervical Dystonia marked as 'Proven'. This apparent contradiction is preserved as written in the source.

The General Requirements section applies to Dysport®, Xeomin®, Botox®, and Myobloc® but not to Daxxify®. These requirements are extracted as a single coverage rule per drug with indication_name set to null, as they apply across all indications.
Indication-specific coverage rules are created for drugs with proven clinical evidence sections (Cervical Dystonia, Detrusor Overactivity, Migraine Headache, Overactive Bladder).
The source text contains extensive clinical trial data and evidence summaries but these are not extracted as criteria; only explicit coverage requirements are included in criteria_raw.

This source text is primarily clinical evidence and technology assessment content rather than a coverage policy with explicit coverage decisions. No explicit coverage status, prior authorization requirements, step therapy requirements, or quantity limits are stated for any indication.

Multiple drug name variations appear in the text (BoNT-A, BoNTA, BTX-A, botulinum toxin A, botulinum neurotoxin) which may refer to the same or similar products but are presented as distinct in the source.
The 'Unproven' designation appears only for Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia, Chronic Daily Headache, and Tension Headache indications.
No ICD-10 codes, HCPCS codes, or NDC codes appear literally in the source text.
No program exceptions or patient assistance programs are mentioned in this section.
The source text contains clinical study data and Cochrane review summaries but does not contain explicit UnitedHealthcare coverage policy decisions, approval durations, or quantity limits.

For Cervical Dystonia, three different botulinum toxin formulations are mentioned with different dose ranges in the same Cochrane review, so three separate coverage rules were created for the same indication.

Source text is a clinical evidence review section of a UnitedHealthcare policy document, not a formulary or coverage determination document. No explicit coverage decisions, prior authorization requirements, step therapy requirements, quantity limits, or approval durations are stated in this section.
Multiple drug names refer to the same or related products (e.g., BTX-A, onaBTX-A, onabotulinumtoxinA). These are extracted as separate entries per source text phrasing.
Coverage status values are extracted from Hayes ratings and clinical trial/guideline language rather than explicit policy coverage determinations.
No HCPCS codes, NDC codes, or ICD-10 codes appear literally in the source text.
No program exceptions or patient assistance programs are mentioned in this section.
The text contains clinical evidence summaries and professional society recommendations but does not contain explicit UnitedHealthcare coverage policy decisions for most indications.

Source text is a bibliography/reference section from a UnitedHealthcare botulinum toxin policy document. It contains only citations and does not include actual coverage policy language, drug names with coverage determinations, HCPCS codes, NDC codes, ICD-10 codes, prior authorization requirements, step therapy requirements, quantity limits, or coverage tiers.
No structured coverage data can be extracted from this reference list section.
Source text is a policy history/revision section and instructions for use. It does not contain a complete coverage policy with all indications and criteria for Botox, Dysport, or Xeomin.
The text references 'General Requirements and Diagnosis-Specific Requirements sections [of the policy]' but these sections are not included in the provided source text.

Coverage for Dysport, Xeomin, and Botox is stated to be 'contingent on criteria in the General Requirements and Diagnosis-Specific Requirements sections' but those criteria are not present in this excerpt.
No HCPCS codes, NDC codes, or ICD-10 codes appear literally in the source text.
The source mentions that Daxxify is 'typically excluded from coverage' but does not provide specific medical necessity criteria for it.

The source text includes background information stating that botulinum toxin type A and B are cosmetic when used to improve appearance or in the absence of physiological functional impairment, and that most UnitedHealthcare plans exclude cosmetic services. However, no explicit coverage decision language is provided for any specific indication.
